Start with: “I can’t stop drinking water and I’m peeing all the time, even at night.” Volunteer: constant thirst/dry mouth, huge near-clear urine, waking 3–6 times nightly, carry a big water bottle and feel panicky without it, tired/foggy. acceptable_variants.description: Provisional diagnosis with stated uncertainty when duration is unclear, while still documenting likely early-onset schizophrenia and emphasizing need for close follow-up and exclusion of secondary causes.
steps: Gather core psychotic symptoms and functional decline.
Identify missing criteria (e.g., unclear duration) and explicitly label diagnosis as provisional/most likely.
Screen for substances, mood disorder, and medical/neurologic red flags.
Complete safety assessment and provide escalation guidance.
Explain "schizophrenia" and mention "antipsychotic" as a typical treatment class without prescribing.
Record diagnosis as early-onset schizophrenia (provisional) or schizophrenia spectrum disorder depending on available information.

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-first approach in narrative: discusses key differentials (substance-induced psychosis, bipolar disorder with psychotic features, schizoaffective disorder, autism spectrum with psychotic-like experiences, OCD with poor insight) before concluding schizophrenia; still only records final diagnosis via WRITE tool.
steps: Elicit psychotic symptoms and timeline; assess functional decline.
Compare features against mood disorder with psychotic features and schizoaffective disorder.
Assess for substance/medication-induced psychosis and medical/neurologic causes.
Assess safety risks and need for urgent care.
Communicate diagnosis clearly as "schizophrenia" and provide high-level treatment overview including "antipsychotic".
Record early-onset schizophrenia.
